data.title: Nasdaq 100 Gap-Down Stalled Above 26,288/142 Key Support, Bulls Are Still In Control
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Risk assets, particularly global equities, staged a sharp bullish reversal on February 8, 2026 following the US-Iran temporary ceasefire, which paused the six-week conflict and raised hopes of a broader peace deal. The high-beta, tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 (NDX) led the rally, reversing an earlier 8% loss to post a 6.9% gain (from the February 27, 2026 pre-war baseline to April 17, 2026) and breaking above its previous all-time high set on October 29, 2025 (see Fig. 1).

Nasdaq 100’s market breadth has improved significantly

Over the past five trading sessions, market breadth within the Nasdaq 100 has strengthened markedly, reinforcing the ongoing bullish trend.

The share of component stocks trading above their 20-day and 50-day moving averages has surged from 11% and 15% on March 27, 2026 to 80% and 62%, respectively, as of April 17, 2026 (see Fig. 2).

Similarly, the proportion of stocks above the key 200-day moving average has risen to 50% from 40% over the same period.

Let's now focus on the short-term trajectory (1-3 days) of the US Nasdaq 100 CFD index and its supporting elements from a technical analysis perspective.

Nasdaq 100 - Gapped down but found support at 26,288/142

The index gapped down by 1.1% at the open of the Asian session on Monday, April 20, 2026, as renewed hostilities in the Strait of Hormuz saw Tehran target vessels and reimpose controls, underscoring a highly fluid US-Iran conflict.

In response, the US Navy engaged and seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship in the Gulf of Oman, casting fresh doubt over the ceasefire set to expire on Tuesday night (US time).

While US Vice President JD Vance is expected to lead another round of peace talks, Iranian state media reports no plans for participation. Notably, the US Nasdaq 100 CFD index managed to stabilise around its former all-time high of 26,288 at this time of writing.

The price actions of the US Nasdaq 100 CFD index (a proxy of the Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures) have continued to oscillate within a minor ascending channel in place from the April 7, 2026 low of 23,808.

Watch the 26,288/26,142 key short-term pivotal support to maintain its minor bullish acceleration phase for the next intermediate resistances to come in at 26,776, 27,140, and 27,380 (see Fig. 3).

On the flip side, a break and an hourly close below 26,142 invalidates the bullish tone for a minor corrective decline to retest the next intermediate support at 25,900/800. Below 25,800 opens scope for a deeper slide towards the 25,215/25,110 medium-term pivotal support area.

Key elements to support the near-term bullish bias on the Nasdaq 100

  • Its price actions have traded above 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages since April 8, 2026. In addition, the 20-day moving average is now shaping a potential bullish crossover condition above the 50-day and 200-day moving averages.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has continued to exhibit a bullish momentum condition above its pull-back support at the 44 level.
  • Elliot Wave Theory suggests the recent rally from the April 2, 2026 low of 23,511 is likely considered as a minor bullish impulsive wave three structure with its potential terminal zone at 27,140/27,380 (Fibonacci extension cluster from and the upper boundary of the minor ascending channel).

Original Post

data.title: lululemon: The Product Is Great, But The Risks Are Greater (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

lululemon athletica inc. (LULU) and I have a very inconsistent relationship. The last time I broke down this company (at the beginning of September 2025), I rated the stock as a Buy and opened a position myself. It came as a great surprise to me, as I couldn’t understand this company from a consumer standpoint. I thought that after looking at the books, I would want to short LULU, but I was wrong, and the numbers together with the valuation multiple were compelling. Here is what I said back then:

This is the first time when my “investor opinion” did not align with my “consumer opinion,” and I realized that while most likely, I won’t ever buy Lululemon’s training pants, I will buy a stock of this company.

Fast forward to April 2026, where I have a full set from lululemon, not just training pants, which I bought shortly after opening a position, just to see for myself what the appeal is. After wearing it many times, I have to say, I absolutely loved it. Whenever I’m in it, everyone asks where I got it from, and I’m happy to share that it was from LULU. My husband also got $180 work trousers for himself last weekend, and he says they're his most comfortable pants for the office.

Everything seems great, except for the fact that after all the events of February and March this year, together with the earnings from lululemon, I can’t say the stock trades at an enormous discount. Now, as I have become an admirer of the clothing, I’m considering closing my position and moving my money into other companies.

I have to be honest, this isn’t a great position to be in, since the stock has made a tiny return in the past 7 months (my average buying price is $163.83, and the price as I’m writing it is $168), but I want to stay realistic. As this experience shows, a great product doesn’t equal a great business, and vice versa. This is a great lesson I learned from investing in this stock, and I won’t be as eager to invest in products I use myself in the future. Now, I prefer relying on my analytical skills in business and finance when I make my investment choices, regardless of what Peter Lynch suggested.

Q4 2025 & Risks

I’ll start with the latest quarter, although this isn’t the only reason for my concerns, but I believe it’s central in combining and confirming all the other arguments and fears. lululemon has managed to beat all the expectations, delivering $5.01 on EPS, $0.23 higher than what was expected, and selling for almost $64 million more than the market hoped for. The stock stayed flat on the news, as this was pretty much the only good news LULU had delivered.

The main concern is that the revenue growth of this company decelerated strongly compared to the growth of previous quarters and had been at only 1% YoY. In my last article, I argued that U.S. growth isn’t the main metric we should consider, especially since international revenue growth is accelerating. This quarter, though, the U.S. revenue started shrinking, bringing a 4% decline in Americas sales. The management attributed it mostly to the additional week in 2024, but also to shifts in consumer preference and other impacts. Even though the company is growing fast in China (24%) and the rest of the world (10%), the Americas is by far the largest segment, with 74% of total net revenue coming from there. If lululemon won’t be able to return to the flat or growing territory, it will be hard for other geographies to offset this drop.

Here, I naturally got to my first major point of concern—geopolitical risks. Even though the Middle Eastern war is on pause for now, and crude oil is below $85, the latest news proves that the situation is far from being stable. It, of course, has a couple of direct and indirect impacts on lululemon. One of the direct impacts is supply chain constraint, which not only boosts the costs but also disrupts the business in the Middle East countries directly, where it has a large presence. It has stores in countries like Bahrain, Israel, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar. During this war, I’ve been to two of these locations—to Dubai in the UAE and Israel, since I reside here.

As far as my observations go (not statistically proven), the sales were negatively impacted only on a limited number of days at the beginning of the war, until businesses got permission to reopen. Since then, people gladly started spending days at the shopping malls, as there is not much to do while you’re a tourist stuck in Dubai or a parent in Israel stuck with a child at home. As a result, I don’t think the demand is the problem, but the inventory limitation. I tried doing shopping myself, calling different shops to ask about specific products, and I was getting “out of inventory” answers more often than I’d like to admit. Of course, we shouldn’t forget about the higher supply chain costs and general difficulty in delivering products from Asia to Europe and the Americas.

The other concern is tied directly to the U.S. The ongoing war intensified my fears over consumer resilience, and all the impacts, together with tariffs from last year, seem to be already impacting this company. While unemployment has improved for the last few quarters, and it seems there are more job openings, there are some additional metrics we should check before drawing our conclusions.

For example, regarding unemployment, I suggest reading Jack Bowman’s latest article on the topic, which shows that the overall situation might not be as bright as the headlines suggest. Here’s what especially interested me:

The headline numbers are flashy because they're directionally correct, but I've been watching another statistic for the last year: job leavers who never re-enter. This subset of folks is simply called “people not in the labor force” or “marginally attached” in the report. If they looked for a job in the last 12 weeks but not in the past 4 weeks before the survey, they also end up in “discouraged workers.” We saw a fairly big jump in this figure, and it was basically equal to all the previously unemployed folks we had. We dropped 332k unemployed people YoY, but we added 325k people to the marginally attached category.

From various other Jack’s macro-focused articles, I know that the situation with job openings is also not very straightforward, as there is a real difficulty for young professionals in getting hired. There could indeed be enough work for school or associate's degree holders, including part-time gigs, but very little proposition for recent university graduates, who should be a core part of Lululemon’s target audience. Moreover, my exposure to people of my age (people 5 years under/over the age of 30) in social media and in real life shows that the layoffs have been really brutal throughout 2025-2026, and there is a real depression when it comes to job hunting, even for people with working experience in major international companies.

I could’ve argued that the management of the company must be aware of all the macro-related risks, but, overlooking all the management-related drama, I can’t make that statement with full confidence. The founder, who is the former CEO, has been very clear about his unhappiness with the current management, to the point that he will be starting another company to compete with LULU. The trajectory of lululemon is truly unclear, and I’ll need to see LULU management's decisions going forward to understand whether the latest CEO's step back has indeed brought any improvements.

Gross and operating margins are decreasing fast as a result of tariffs, although their gross margin is still at 54.9%, which is better than most retail manufacturers. The sector median for the gross margin is at ~39%, which means LULU is doing 45% better than the sector on this metric. NIKE, Inc. (NKE), for example, has a gross margin of 40%, which gives it less breathing room in absorbing risks like tariffs, but still, shrinking margins unnerve the market, and it is expected of the company to get it under control. Considering the difficulties with costs and supply chain, together with the consumer sentiment, getting margins under control could be an almost impossible goal to achieve.

The good news is that the company has a lot of cash, and management is convinced that the stock is oversold at the current valuation multiples. In Q4 2025, lululemon repurchased 1.4 million shares for $269 million. Besides, insider trading shows not only selling but also buying:

lululemon Chief Brand Officer Nicole Neuburger sold 622 shares at $161 per share for $100,142, lowering her holdings by 3.14% to 19,156 shares.

lululemon director Charles Bergh purchased 6,090 shares at $164.20 per share for $999,978, increasing his stake to 6,362 shares.

Valuation

Valuation is another bright side, and it is the reason I’ll most likely keep my position for a little longer. I think I still have the time to analyze new decisions of the management, and to be fair, there are things I can connect to. For example, the management says they’ll try boosting the sales for the full price, which could turn out to be a serious revenue boost if successful.

We are focused on the growth of our full-price business. Looking at 2026, a primary goal in North America is returning the business to healthier levels of full-price sales after seeing a higher markdown penetration in 2025. We are already seeing better full-price sell-through in Q1 relative to Q4, and we are targeting further improvement as we move through the year, driven by increased product newness, innovation, and operating discipline.

In this industry, discounts are so common that LULU risks being avoided by the customer, but at the same time, if successful, rivals could also try to cut off the discounts, which could be a powerful catalyst for the whole industry if the economy stays resilient.

Returning to the valuation, even though the risks are real, and they are not going to disappear any time soon, a lot of them have already been priced in before. The company trades at 13.5 times forward price to earnings, and it had never been so low until recently. It trades 20% to 40% cheaper than the sector, while many of the headwinds and negative catalysts are related to the entire sector. It means that lululemon indeed could have been oversold as a result of the incredibly high premium it used to have in the past. Another argument, which suggests the stock is at the bottom, is the fact that it didn’t drop much further after the recent quarter, which was definitely not great.

The same, as I mentioned in my last piece, I’ll mention it here—I don’t think the company deserved to be as expensive as it used to, so I’m not hoping for it to return to the old levels. Those days are most likely over, unless something huge happens.

Conclusion

For the conclusion, I have to say I’m hesitant about whether to continue holding this stock or to rotate my money elsewhere. The fact that the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 Index (SP500) since I opened my position doesn’t add confidence. On the other hand, lululemon trades at an enormous discount not only to its past levels but also to the overall sector, which goes through many of the same issues. It could mean that a patient shareholder might benefit well in the future, but that most likely won’t happen this year.

As for myself, I think I’ll trim my position to keep a smaller exposure, but I won’t close my position entirely, as I still want to see how lululemon develops over the upcoming quarters. This decision is related mostly to the industry concerns, as if the war continues, retail will be one of the industries to feel the impact more strongly than others in the U.S. But even if the war stops, enough headwinds are weighing on lululemon and its rivals in 2026, without too many positive catalysts in sight. For this reason, I rate the stock as a Hold, as I won't be increasing my position at this moment.

data.title: Polaris: Now Advantaged By Domestic Manufacturing Capacity
data.description:
Polaris Inc. (PII) has had significant volatility in the past couple of days due to a competitor's abrupt guidance withdrawal amid changing tariff policies. Luckily, Polaris isn't affected nearly as much by tariff changes due to domestic manufacturing capacity—the company's 2026 guidance remains intact. Polaris' earnings outlook is still relatively weak due to a slow demand environment.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous October 2025 article on the stock, titled “Polaris: Indian Motorcycle Separation Is Positive.” The stock has since lost -15% of its value; meanwhile, the S&P 500 has returned 7%.

Polaris Is Positioned Well Against Change in Tariff Structure

There has been significant volatility in Polaris' sector in the past couple of days. The catalyst for high volatility is driven by tariff policy changes; competitor BRP Inc. (DOO) abruptly withdrew its FY2027 guidance in mid-April due to a change in Section 232 metal tariffs. The concern quickly spread across the sector, notably leading Polaris' stock to decline very sharply in tandem with BRP. I wrote about BRP's guidance withdrawal in a separate article.

In essence, the structure of Section 232 metal tariffs was changed in early April. BRP noted that the company now has to pay a 25% tariff on the total value of imported products instead of a 50% tariff on only the applicable metal content, causing a massive $500 million in earnings pressure for the competitor.

Positively, Polaris doesn't seem to be affected as much as could be expected; Polaris also has to import outsourced parts and manufactures in Mexico and Europe, which would lead to the belief that the company is affected more similarly to BRP. Instead, Polaris issued a statement where the company outlines that tariff policy changes don't have a material impact on the company's 2026 guidance. Polaris has significant manufacturing capacity in the U.S., with a total of three domestic manufacturing plants, covering domestic demand much better than BRP's domestic capacity. The issued statement alleviated significant margin worries.

The tariff shift could now even shift Polaris into a net benefactor of tariffs; BRP faces margin pressure that Polaris doesn't, improving Polaris' comparative pricing power against the major competitor. Other manufacturers also likely have a larger added tariff impact than Polaris; domestic manufacturing capacity is now a clear competitive advantage if the current tariff regime lasts. Ultimately, as tariff policies have continued to shift constantly during the past year, I don't believe that current tariff policies should be taken as a given.

Polaris' outlook is still quite weak. Sales are guided to grow by 1-3% in 2026 to $7.2-7.4 billion from a low comparison level. Retail demand remains subdued; high interest rates and low consumer confidence clearly continue to weigh on demand for recreational vehicles. Polaris assumes retail demand to stay flat. The adjusted EBITDA margin is guided to expand by 80 to 120 basis points and is still expected to remain at quite a thin 6.5-6.9% level. Polaris started to report a starting recovery in the second half of 2025, but further progress is likely to be low due to volatile macroeconomic conditions.

First quarter results will be reported on the 28th of April. Wall Street expects revenues to come in at $1.64 billion with 6.8% year-on-year growth and that the adjusted EPS will improve by $0.49 year-on-year to a -$0.41 loss for the seasonally slow quarter. Momentum from the past couple of quarters should still last into Q1, but a volatile macroeconomic environment will likely start to cause more turbulence again afterward.

PII Stock Has a Fair Margin of Safety

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Polaris. I again expect sales growth to be elevated through the 2027-2032 period as industry demand recovers, but that sales growth ultimately stabilizes at 2% alongside inflation.

Likewise, I now estimate the EBIT margin to recover to a 9% level by 2030, reflecting a level more in line with Polaris' performance in normal industry conditions.

Polaris' cash flow conversion should be fairly good after taxes.

The estimates put Polaris' fair value at $69.5, suggesting 20% upside as of writing. The stock has some upside potential, but as the timeline for an earnings recovery is uncertain, such estimated upside, in my opinion, represents a very fair margin of safety. Improved earnings visibility, or a lower stock price, could make the stock an attractive investment.

The fair value estimate is down from $74.5 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 10.01%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate an 8.09% interest rate, annualized from Q4 debt and interest expenses. I again expect quite a high 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.31% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran's most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.68. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity stands at 11.58% and the WACC at 10.01%.

Takeaway

The forward outlook is still very volatile for Polaris. Recent tariff changes haven't affected the company nearly as much as BRP, even potentially creating a cost advantage, but constant tariff changes and other macroeconomic uncertainty continue to cause turbulence. High interest rates and low consumer confidence weigh on consumer demand.

PII stock is valued with a fair margin of safety, and as such, I remain at a Hold rating.

data.title: Digital Turbine: Deep Value Buy Opportunity With Profitable Ad-Tech Trends
data.description:
I previously rated Digital Turbine, Inc. (APPS) as a Buy in November 2025, thanks to the renewed profitable growth prospects from the higher smart device sales and the expanding OEM/carrier partnerships.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating here, thanks to its deep value investment thesis from the prior meltdown.

APPS Delivers Improved Monetization Trends

Since my last buy rating, APPS has underperformed given the market-wide SaaS/tech pessimism through March 2026, as similarly observed in its ad-tech peers in varying degrees.

I am of the opinion that APPS' meltdown has been unwarranted, albeit triggering an improved entry point for those looking to dollar cost average.

1. Advertising Growth Flywheel

For one, APPS operates across two core segments, namely the:

  • On Device Solutions [ODS] - the delivery of mobile application media/content/application for advertiser/publisher customers through mobile carriers/device OEMs, and
  • App Growth Platform [AGP] - the monetization of users through its end-to-end advertising capability across Supply-Side Platform [SSP] and Demand-Side Platform [DSP], along with the provision of "brand and performance advertising products to advertisers and agencies."

In short, APPS offers "over 80,000 different applications" through Ignite, its app distribution platform, while allowing advertisers access to up to 3B of its installed devices - with it triggering a rather powerful growth flywheel across app distribution and ad monetization, respectively.

Thanks to its expanded carrier/OEM/mobile game developer partnerships, APPS has been able to report a robust ODS revenue growth to $99.55M in FQ3'26 (+8.5% YoY), particularly in APAC/China by +50.9% YoY thanks to the "strong volumes from our international partners."

The higher installed base then allows the ad-tech company to deliver a robust AGP revenue growth to $52.61M (+18.9% YoY), thanks to the "higher advertiser demand, which translated into improving pricing and fill rates, particularly for premium placements on our platform."

Part of their growth tailwinds are also attributed to the management's ongoing diversification beyond "strictly gaming inventory and increased non-gaming inventory," allowing them to tap into an expanded advertiser base, particularly in the retail sector, which has delivered "5x growth compared to last holiday season as our retail media efforts are bearing fruit with large retailers wanting to extend their audiences."

With these developments already triggering the higher revenue-per-device trends, it is unsurprising then, that APPS has reported the richer gross profit margin of 49% (+5 points YoY), aided by the lower revenue share paid to its carrier/OEM partners.

Combined with the utilization of AI/machine learning tools on their first-party data and the consequently, higher advertising ROI (aided by the lower headcounts), the ad-tech company has been able to report an improved operating leverage at an adj. EBITDA margin of 25.6% as well (+9.3 points YoY).

2. Alternative App Distribution

Beyond the duopoly of the app distribution platforms through Apple's (AAPL) AppStore, and Alphabet's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Play Store, the recent court ruling already allows for alternative app distribution/payment methods and for many publishers to enjoy new app discovery platforms.

Given that the original lawsuit arose from Epic Games introducing "a direct billing mechanism to its video game, Fortnite, on iPhone and Android," it is unsurprising that APPS already reports significant "progress with 3 of the largest global mobile game developers signed in the December quarter now using Single-Tap capabilities in their alternative distribution efforts."

With the alternative distribution platform already live and generating revenue as of February 2026, I am of the opinion that APPS may stand to benefit from their expanded revenue sharing opportunities from FQ4'26 onwards.

These reasons may also be why the management has felt confident to raise their FY2026 guidance across:

  • revenues of $555.5M at the midpoint (+13.2% YoY) and
  • adj EBITDA of $115.5M (+59.7% YoY),

albeit somewhat paling in comparison against the YTD outperformance at +13.8% YoY and +75.7% YoY, respectively.

With APPS expected to report their FQ4'26 earnings call on May 22, 2026 (Post-Market), I urge readers to monitor their performance and FY2027 guidance, albeit with the thrice-raised FY2026 guidance boding well for their reversal to growth prospects.

This is after the worst of the growth headwinds observed between FY2023 and FY2025 (arising from reduced ad spending, lower device volumes, and exit from certain legacy AdColony business lines, amongst others).

If anything, the same quiet optimism has also been embedded in the consensus forward estimates, with APPS expected to report excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +12.3%/+33.6% through FY2027.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at the 5Y CAGR of +28.7%/+11.8% and the 5Y pre-pandemic levels of +37.5%/NA, respectively.

In the meantime, I urge readers to monitor the currently impacted smartphone shipment trends by -6% YoY in Q1'26, since the "DRAM and NAND shortages disrupted supplies and increased costs across OEMs, while consumer sentiment remained weak amid Middle East tensions."

These imply near-term growth headwinds for APPS, with it likely to trigger the management's cautious FY2027 guidance in the upcoming FQ4'26 earnings call.

3. APPS' Promising Reversal Is Cheaply Valued

Thanks to the -54.7% correction from the 52-week high and the consensus raised forward estimates, it is unsurprising that APPS is a lot more compelling at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 6.77x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 9.80x.

This is down from the 5Y mean of 14.71x/17.68x and the sector median of 13.92x/23.37x, respectively.

When comparing APPS' FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 6.77x and the YTD adj EBITDA margins of 21.5% (+7.6 points YoY) against its fellow DSP/SSP peers, including:

  • DSP/SSP and hyperscaler/commerce - Amazon (AMZN) at 13.06x/20.3% in FY2025,
  • SSP - Magnite (MGNI) at 7.85x/32.5% in FY2025,
  • DSP/SSP and hyperscaler - GOOG at 18.59x/37.2% in FY2025,
  • DSP - The Trade Desk (TTD) at 7.33x/41.1% in FY2025,
  • DSP/SSP and social media - Meta (META) at 12.40x/50.6% in FY2025, and
  • DSP/SSP - AppLovin (APP) at 24.01x/82.2% in FY2025, respectively,

it is apparent that APPS remains rather attractively valued compared to its more profitable ad-tech peers, thanks to the meltdown.

This is especially since APPS has been able to report a healthier balance sheet at a net debt position of $314.54M in FQ3'26 (+15.6% YoY) and a notably lower net debt to adj. EBITDA ratio of 2.72x (compared to a year ago at 5.15x) - partly attributed to the richer free cash flow generation/adj. EBITDA.

This is albeit with an equity dilution risk, as the management also leverages a capital raise of $58.56M on a YTD basis and therefore contributes to the notably higher shares outstanding of 120.47M in FQ3'26 (+15.6% YoY).

As a result of the mixed optics from the top/bottom-line reversal against the shareholder equity erosion, I can understand why APPS has underperformed some of its ad-tech peers thus far.

Otherwise, the management has signaled at their ability to sustainably fund their future operations, likely through free cash flow generation, given the terminated "existing at-the-market equity program."

These developments bode well for APPS' profitable growth prospects, despite their lack of long-term guidance.

APPS Is An Even Better Buy After The Dip

For now, the bulls have seemingly defended APPS' new trading floor at the $2.80s during the worst of the March/April 2026 selloff, with the stock seemingly on its way to retest the next resistance levels of $3.70s in the near term.

Based on the discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 9.80x and the LTM adj EPS performance of $0.48 (+29.7% sequentially), it is apparent that the stock is trading below my base-case fair value estimates of $4.70.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $0.63, there remains an excellent upside potential of +62% to my intermediate term price target of $6.10, if not more, assuming the speculative upward re-rating nearer to APPS' 5Y P/E mean of 17.68x or even, 10Y mean of 25x (not too far from the peer group mean of 21.30x and the sector median of 23.37x).

While APPS is likely to remain volatile in the near term, attributed to the elevated short interest ratio of 11.92% at the time of writing, I am of the opinion that its risk/reward profile is even more compelling here, aided by the technical bottoming in early April 2026.

Combined with the robust YTD performance, the thrice-raised FY2026 guidance, the notably healthier balance sheet, and the discounted valuations, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the APPS stock here.

data.title: Xcelerate Your Portfolio's Returns With Xcel Energy
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

In the world of income investing, Dividend Aristocrats are often considered to be the blue-chip standard. That's because they represent a select group of companies that meet the following two requirements:

  1. They must be S&P 500 index (SP500) constituents, which requires large-cap market capitalization, as well as trading volume liquidity minimums.
  2. They must raise their dividends for at least 25 consecutive years, which is a very strong filter. This is because a company's business model must be sound enough to navigate a variety of environments (recessions, pandemics, and wars) to fuel a growing payout.

That brings me to my focus for today, which is Xcel Energy (XEL). When Kody last covered it with a Buy rating in January, he was encouraged by its updated five-year capital spending plan. He also thought it had the financial capacity to make these plans a reality. Topping off the Buy case, shares were moderately discounted.

Three months later, I'm reiterating his Buy rating. XEL's $60 billion capital spending plan continues to support high single-digit percentage annual ongoing diluted EPS growth potential. Recent deals show the regulated utility is balancing strategic risk management (playing defense) with growth ambitions (going on offense). XEL remains financially stable, too. Finally, shares are still priced below my fair value estimate.

Executing on Its Growth Vision

On February 5th, XEL shared its earnings report for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2025. The company's operating revenue rose by 14.1% over the year-ago period to $3.56 billion in the quarter. For more perspective, that came up $220 million short of Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus estimate for the quarter.

What was behind XEL's topline surge for the fourth quarter? Growth in electric operating revenue was the driving factor in the quarter (+16.6% to $2.81 billion). That was made possible by a combination of a significant increase in electricity demand from large-load customers and regulatory rate case recoveries during the quarter. The company began seeing the revenue impact of several data center projects ramping up, particularly in the SPS (Texas/New Mexico) and PSCo (Colorado) service territories. In Minnesota and Colorado, XEL received authorization to increase rates to recover billions in infrastructure investments made over the prior 24 months. These infrastructure riders and base rate increases helped the utility to earn a regulated return on the $12 billion that it invested in 2025 alone.

The lesser growth driver for XEL was a 6% year-over-year increase in its natural gas operating revenue to $737 million for the fourth quarter. This was mostly due to higher natural gas costs (+16% to $333 million) in the quarter, which the regulated utility can pass on to customers. All the while, profitability is unchanged because the higher rates are a dollar-for-dollar offset for greater resulting operating expenses.

XEL's ongoing diluted EPS jumped 18.5% higher over the year-ago period to $0.96 during the fourth quarter. That was in line with the analyst consensus for the quarter. Disciplined operating expense management (+7.5% to $2.98 billion) led ongoing diluted EPS growth to outpace operating revenue growth in the quarter.

Looking to the next release due on April 30th, the consensus analyst expectation is $0.92 per share, lower than the prior quarter but higher than $0.83 per share from Q1 of 2025. We don't expect any major surprises outside of the consensus estimate, as historically, XEL has been extremely reliable in its earnings potential. XEL has a clear historical pattern of seasonality, where Q1 and Q2 are smaller earnings quarters while Q3 and Q4 are stronger.

XEL's fundamentals remain vigorous. The company has more than 2 GW of data center capacity under construction, with the projects either online already or set to come online later this year. The ramp-ups to full capacity for these projects will be later this decade and into the next decade. Then, there's another 4 GW of capacity with hyperscalers and large developers in advanced discussions that are expected to be online by 2027 through 2030, with ramp-ups through the mid-2030s. Lastly, XEL has an additional 20+ GW in its early-stage pipeline.

That immense pipeline of data center projects served as the impetus for a strategic move to expand its strategic alliance with NextEra Energy (NEE). The two companies are co-developing generation storage and interconnections to serve data center projects across their operating companies. This will help better anticipate system needs for new data centers, streamline development timelines, and advance innovative grid technologies, according to Chairman and CEO Bob Frenzel's opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call.

In February, XEL also announced a reservation agreement with GE Vernova (GEV) for five F-class gas turbines. This move will effectively support the regulated utility's generation and grid projects into the 2030s, which reduces the long-term supply chain risks to its capital spending plan.

One final encouraging development for XEL that served as the latest reminder of its remarkable growth story was its deal struck with Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) to power a new 1.9 GW Google data center in Pine Island, Minnesota.

XEL is also financially sturdy. The company's FFO-to-debt ratio was 16% in 2025, and this is expected to hold over the next few years before improving further to 17% in 2029 and beyond. XEL possesses a BBB S&P credit rating and Baa1 (BBB+ equivalent) and BBB+ credit ratings from Moody's and Fitch, respectively. These provide it with the low cost of capital needed to execute its capital spending plan.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for XEL's ongoing diluted EPS to grow by 8.4% in 2026 to $4.12. In 2027, another 10% rise in ongoing diluted EPS to $4.53 is anticipated. For 2028, an additional 9.5% increase in ongoing diluted EPS to $4.96 is being predicted (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to XEL's Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and XEL's March 2026 Investor Presentation).

Fair Value Could Be Around $85 A Share

Year-to-date, XEL has generated a 10.5% total return. This was a substantial outperformance versus the 4.1% total return posted by the S&P 500 index over that time. And while the regulated utility isn't quite the value that it was in January, it still looks buyable here.

At the current $81 share price (as of April 17th, 2026), XEL's forward 12-month P/E ratio is 19. That's a full turn higher than the prior value of 17.8. Still, it's less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 20.6 (up a bit from the prior value of 20.4), per data from FAST Graphs.

Looking ahead, I'm sticking with Kody's fair value P/E ratio of roughly 20. What's my rationale for this valuation multiple?

On the one hand, the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) of 4.3% is meaningfully greater than the 10-year average of 2.7%, according to data from Macrotrends. In the coming years, I believe that the 10-year yield will hover around the low to mid-4% range.

On the other hand, I would argue that this elevated interest rate environment is mostly offset by a substantially stronger growth profile than in previous years. XEL's 9.3% annual forward ongoing diluted EPS growth consensus is higher than the previous figure of 8.9%. What's more, this would represent a significant acceleration from the 10-year CAGR of 6.2%.

That's why I agree with Kody's assessment that XEL's earnings yield relative to the 10-year yield will narrow from a 200 to 250 basis point spread down to a roughly 100 basis point spread (the latter of which has already been the typical spread in recent years). This reflects the company's vastly better growth prospects than five or 10 years ago.

Applying my fair value multiple of 20, I compute a fair value of $85 a share. Against the current $81 share price, that's equivalent to a 5% discount to fair value.

If XEL matches growth forecasts and returns to fair value, it could deliver an 8% total return by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, it could produce nearly 10% annual total returns.

Dividend Aristocrat Status on the Horizon

XEL's 2.9% forward dividend yield registers around 20 basis points below the utility sector's 3.1% median forward yield. That's a roughly 20 basis point narrowing of the spread since January. This is what earns XEL C+ and C grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (both of which are up from C and C- grades in January).

Despite its otherwise ordinary starting yield, the regulated utility sets itself apart in a couple of other areas. Having just raised its dividend for the 23rd consecutive year, XEL's dividend growth streak is more than double the sector median of just 9.4 years. That's sufficient for the Quant System to issue an A grade for overall dividend consistency (the same as it was in January).

Then, there's the fact that XEL's payout is reasonably safe. The company's ongoing diluted EPS payout ratio is poised to be in the high-50% range in 2026 (XEL plans on lowering the payout ratio further to a targeted range of between 45% and 55% to more sustainably fund its considerable growth capex). This would be much lower than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies set forth as their upper limits for a sustainable dividend, per the Zen Research Terminal.

Consequently, the Zen Research Terminal puts the probability of a dividend cut at 0.7% within the next 12 months. In the next average recession, this risk remains limited at 2.75%. That reinforces the B+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety (down from an A- in January).

The Quant System anticipates 4.2% annual forward dividend per share growth. That would be about 110 basis points below the sector median forecast of 5.3% (a 50-basis-point widening of the spread versus January). This projection makes sense because XEL's dividend growth will have to lag ongoing diluted EPS growth over the next several years to bring it down to the midpoint of its targeted range. That's why the Quant System awards a C- grade for the forward dividend per share growth metric (down from a C grade in January).

Risks to Consider

XEL is a regulated utility with a robust growth story. However, it faces risks that could put pressure on the investment thesis.

As Kody outlined in January, the company is highly concentrated with its NSP-Minnesota (including modest operations in North Dakota and South Dakota) and Public Service Company of Colorado businesses. Together, the two businesses comprise approximately 77% of XEL's $55.8 billion rate base as of December 31st, 2025 (page 9 of 147 of XEL's 10-K Filing). The remaining 23% of the company's rate base comes from Southwestern Public Service (Texas and New Mexico) and NSP-Wisconsin (Wisconsin and Michigan).

This makes future rate case settlements in Minnesota and Colorado critically important to XEL's fundamentals. Adverse rate case outcomes in either of these markets (e.g., the company doesn't receive the majority of its requested rate increases) could potentially result in credit rating downgrades from the rating agencies. This could undermine XEL's current $60 billion five-year capital spending plan.

Such a scenario is worth watching because the macroenvironment is becoming riskier for regulated utilities. An AARP survey of more than 1,000 Americans aged 50 and older from last April reinforces this point: It found that at least three out of four respondents were worried about the cost of their utilities rising, with 83% concerned about higher electricity costs. Pressure from these more politically active constituents could make unfavorable rate case decisions from state regulators more likely as the years unfold.

Another risk that Kody highlighted recently was the potential for natural disaster risks, especially with high wildfire risks in Texas and Colorado. A recent report from Verisk Wildfire Analytics ranked these states as the most dangerous for wildfires in the nation, alongside California. The company is taking many mitigative measures with $5 billion in planned spending for situational awareness tools, system hardening, and Public Safety Power Shutoff drills. Still, the risk of billions of dollars of wildfire damage in its service territories remains in play.

Another risk to XEL is its unionized workforce. As of December 31st, 2025, 44% of employees (5,036) were covered by collective bargaining agreements (page 7 of 147 of XEL's 10-K Filing). Any inability to resolve labor disputes before contract expirations would put XEL at risk of interrupted operations. Renewing collective bargaining agreements in the future could require meaningful boosts in wages and benefits for employees. That could create a lag period between when XEL incurs these higher expenses and when it recovers them in future rate cases, which could at least temporarily crimp profit margins.

Conclusion

XEL's expanded deal with NEE backs up data center growth, ensuring that new large-load customers pay their fair share, protecting existing residential and small business customers from bearing the infrastructure costs. The agreement with GEV provides it with the hardware that it needs for its capital spending plan. The recent deal with Google for another data center is the latest win that will power future growth. XEL's balance sheet remains investment-grade as well.

Finally, shares are trading at a slight discount to my fair value estimate. That paves the way for double-digit percentage annual total returns from a soon-to-be Dividend Aristocrat, which is why I'm standing by my Buy rating.

data.title: Conestoga Capital Advisors Q1 2026 Market Review
data.description:
Market Review

The first quarter of 2026 began with equity markets trading just below the all-time highs reached late last year, but sentiment shifted meaningfully as the quarter progressed. Early optimism around improving domestic economic conditions and attractive Small Cap valuations gave way to renewed volatility following geopolitical developments in the Middle East and a significant repricing of interest rate expectations.

Small Cap equities initially performed well, benefiting from improving earnings expectations and their greater sensitivity to U.S. economic activity. However, that momentum faded in February as rising energy prices and heightened geopolitical uncertainty introduced a more cautious tone across global markets. At the same time, expectations for Federal Reserve easing shifted materially. Investors began the year anticipating roughly 50 basis points of rate cuts in 2026, but by quarter-end those expectations had largely been priced out. The resulting adjustment in interest rate expectations contributed to a steepening yield curve and tighter financial conditions at the margin.

Against this backdrop, the Russell 2000 Index rose nearly 9% through late January before retracing into correction territory by early March, ultimately finishing the quarter modestly higher at +0.89%. Despite this elevated price volatility, Small Cap equities outperformed Large Caps by more than five percentage points. In our view, this suggests the long-anticipated broadening of market leadership beyond Mega Cap stocks may be delayed rather than derailed, as some have feared.

Market leadership in the quarter came from a narrow subset of stocks, much like it did in 2025, with higher-beta momentum stocks driving the gains. From a sector perspective, Energy was far and away the strongest performer, followed by Basic Materials and Industrials. In fact, Energy’s gains in 1Q26 were the fourth-best quarterly relative performance by a leading sector since 1985. (Source: FRP, FactSet, as of 3/31/26.)

In contrast, investor sentiment toward software companies weakened as markets increasingly focused on the potential for AI-driven disruption to compress traditional software moats and long-term growth expectations. This shift in sentiment has occurred in spite of ongoing solid operating performance across much of the sector.

Looking ahead, markets remain sensitive to geopolitical developments and the trajectory of energy prices and inflation. In this type of environment, our view is that retaining exposure to companies which we believe demonstrate durable, high-quality characteristics (e.g., higher ROIC, strong balance sheets, and consistent free cash flow) remains important.

Performance (Total Net Returns as of 3/31/26)

1Q26 1 Year 3 Years 5 Years 10 Years Since Inception 12/31/1998
Conestoga Small Cap Composite (net) -5.01% -3.69% 0.54% -1.35% 9.52% 10.12%
Russell 2000 Growth Index -2.81% 23.58% 12.27% 1.62% 9.79% 7.10%
Since 1/31/2017
Conestoga SMid Cap Composite (net) -10.24% -9.27% 3.11% -0.58% 9.82%
Russell 2500 Growth Index -3.52% 19.31% 10.61% 1.75% 9.74%
Since 12/31/2019
Conestoga Micro Cap Composite (net) -7.14% 17.64% 5.63% -3.02% 8.10%
Russell Microcap Growth Index -4.25% 41.85% 15.49% -1.21% 7.17%

All periods longer than one year are annualized. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Returns are shown net of actual investment management fees and reflect the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Gross returns are available upon request. Performance is presented as supplemental information, please see Important Information at the end of this commentary. The Russell 2000 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. The Russell 2500 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2500 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. The Russell Microcap Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell Microcap companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. (Sources: Conestoga, Russell Investments.)

Firm Update

We are pleased to announce that Zach Weiss has joined the firm as an Equity Analyst and is responsible for fundamental research and idea generation across the firm’s Small, SMid, and Micro Cap strategies. Prior to joining Conestoga, Zach was a Senior Analyst at Royce Investment Partners in New York from 2022 to 2026, a specialist in Small Cap investing, where he focused on research and idea generation supporting the firm’s Premier Quality strategy. Earlier in his career, Zach was a Research Analyst at FBB Capital Partners from 2019 to 2022 in the Washington, D.C. area, where he worked alongside the Director of Research managing equity portfolios and contributed to the launch of a Small Cap strategy. Zach began his career as an Equity Research Associate at B. Riley FBR from 2018 to 2019 covering Small Cap companies and earlier worked in audit at PwC. Zach received a BA in Finance and Accounting from James Madison University. He is a CFA Institute Charterholder, CPA, and holds the CMT designation.

As of March 31, 2026, Conestoga’s total assets were $5.8 Billion. Assets within our three primary institutional investment strategies were:

$3.9 Bil

Small Cap Growth

$1.8 Bil

SMid Cap Growth

$51 Mil

Micro Cap Growth

Strong Portfolio Fundamentals Despite Significant Valuation Compression in High Quality Stocks

Recent performance has been driven primarily by multiple compression rather than a deterioration in underlying business fundamentals. As shown below in the left-hand chart, high quality small cap equities are currently trading at or near historically low relative valuation levels, following a period of broad-based multiple contraction. In the last 30+ years, there have been only two other periods where relative valuations have been this low.

At the same time, Conestoga’s portfolio companies have continued to deliver strong sales and EBITDA growth relative to the Russell 2000 Growth benchmark as depicted in the right-hand chart. While the timing of a recovery is uncertain, similar valuation dislocations have historically been followed by periods of meaningful multiple expansion, particularly when supported by stable fundamentals.

There are no guarantees valuations will revert or that multiples will expand. The Relative Enterprise Value-to-Cash Flow (EV/CF) ratio measures a company’s total value (including debt) relative to its cash-generating ability, facilitating comparisons against peers. EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) growth measures the increase in a company's earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization over a specific period, signaling improved operational efficiency and profitability. It is a key metric for assessing core business performance, often used to determine valuation multiples and cash flow generation. The Russell 2000 Index is a stock market index that measures the performance of approximately 2,000 of the smallest publicly traded companies. The Russell 2000 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. The S&P SmallCap 600 Index (S&P 600) is a market-capitalization-weighted index managed by S&P Dow Jones Indices that tracks 600 small-cap U.S. companies, serving as a benchmark for the small-cap segment. An individual cannot invest directly in an index.

Material Risks:

Investing involves risk, including possible loss of principal. Micro, Small, and SMid cap stocks may be more volatile and less liquid than larger companies. Growth stocks may be more sensitive to changes in interest rates and investor sentiment and may experience valuation compression. Sector exposures may increase volatility. Benchmark comparisons may differ due to holdings, fees, and other factors.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ManpowerGroup: Still Being Patient On This Stock
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on ManpowerGroup Inc. (MAN) was a hold rating because profitability remained weak despite organic growth showing two straight quarters of recovery. After looking at the Q1 2026 results, I still think a hold rating makes sense. The better part of this quarter was that the revenue recovery looks more credible now, with improvement across France, Italy, Southern Europe, and the Manpower brand. That said, the earnings side still does not look clean enough. Gross profit fell, gross margin missed guidance, and the strongest demand is still coming from lower-margin enterprise customers. My fear is that consensus estimates are too high and MAN misses expectations, which would very likely weigh on the stock valuation.

1Q26 results update

MAN saw Q1 2026 revenue of ~$4.51 billion, up 10.3% y/y on a reported basis. On a constant-currency basis, revenue grew 2.9% y/y. Note that organic days-adjusted constant-currency revenue grew 3% in the quarter, and systemwide revenue, which includes franchise revenue, was $5 billion.

By geography, Americas revenue saw $1.12 billion, up 3.5% y/y in constant currency. Within that, US revenue came in at $655 million, down 5% on a days-adjusted basis. Southern Europe revenue saw $2.1 billion, up 3% y/y in constant currency; France revenue saw $1.1 billion and was flat in constant currency, while Italy revenue was $475 million and was up 8% on a days-adjusted constant-currency basis. Northern Europe revenue was $790 million, down 1% in organic constant currency. Finally, Asia Pacific Middle East revenue saw $510.5 million, up 8.1% y/y in constant currency, and within that, Japan revenue grew 4% on a days-adjusted constant-currency basis. By brand, Manpower revenue grew 6% y/y in organic constant currency, up from 5% growth in Q4 2025. Experis revenue declined 9% y/y, worse than the 6% decline in Q4 2025, mainly due to the timing of healthcare IT projects in the US. Talent Solutions revenue declined 1% y/y, which was better than the 4% decline in Q4 2025.

As for profitability, gross profit saw $723 million, up 3.5% y/y on a reported basis but down 2.8% in constant currency. Margin-wise, gross margin was 16%, down ~100 bps y/y, with Staffing margin reducing gross margin by 70 bps due to mix shifts and bench utilization, permanent recruitment reducing margin by 20 bps, and other services reducing margin by another 20 bps. The good news was that adj. SG&A fell 4% y/y in constant currency and now represents ~15% of revenue, down from ~16% in Q1 2025. All in all, this led to net earnings of $2.5 million, or $0.05 per diluted share on a GAAP basis. But adjusting for restructuring and strategic transformation program costs, adjusted EPS was $0.51.

The revenue recovery story is getting more credible

A few months ago, the main question I had was whether MAN had simply stopped getting worse or whether a real turnaround had started. I think this Q1 2026 performance made it clear that MAN is turning around. Q1 marked the fifth straight quarter of year-over-year revenue trend improvement for organic days-adjusted constant-currency revenue (up 3%). What's important is where the improvement came from. France finally got back to flat (was down 2% in Q4 2025); Italy's growth accelerated to 8%; and Southern Europe continued to print positive growth of 3%. Management also said manufacturing conditions are getting better across Europe and the US, with improving PMI trends and good momentum in countries like Italy and Spain. The numbers also suggest demand momentum is improving, as the Manpower brand (mainly for industrial and manufacturing hiring) saw growth acceleration sequentially, by 100 bps to 6%. Broadly, the idea here is that recovery is showing up across several places, and that makes the growth setup much more attractive.

I should also add that the strongest demand seen in Q1 came from enterprise customers. I view this very positively because large clients usually move first when hiring conditions start to improve. So, while the mix is not ideal for margin, it is still a useful signal that demand is coming back. We have actually seen this happening in Q1, and now with two quarters of this happening, I believe there is real hiring activity that is backing this recovery story. Importantly, management said pricing remains rational, which suggests MAN is not buying growth through weaker pricing.

There is also a second leg to the story now. MAN is structurally lowering its cost base. So far, work has started from the back office, which I expect to help remove some overhead and standardize support work, which means some savings can start showing up in 2026. The front-office redesign is probably more important because it impacts how MAN actually sells, recruits, and serves clients. My view is that if recruiters can source, screen, and match candidates faster, and if sales teams can target demand better, MAN should be able to support more revenue without growing headcount by as much. A good example is the AI sales targeting tool in France, which has already generated ~$200 million of incremental revenue. MAN has also completed >25,000 AI-led interviews in the past six months and reduced screening time by 67%. If this goes on, it should naturally lead to margin expansion, which solves the concern I had about profitability.

Valuation

The valuation looks optically cheap vs. peers and its history, but I think that may be misleading if consensus is too aggressive on adj. EPS. Consensus is already pricing in an acceleration in adj. EPS growth, as you can see in the table below. Yes, the revenue growth trend is improving, and that has helped sentiment. But I am not confident that adj. EPS will grow as fast as consensus expects. The reality is gross profit fell despite positive organic growth, gross margin missed guidance, the enterprise mix is still weighing on profitability (despite this being a topline contributor), and permanent hiring remains soft.

I would prefer to wait for another 1 or 2 quarters of MAN showing strong adj. EPS growth before I'm convinced that the topline growth story is translating to earnings growth.

Conclusion

My view on MAN is still a hold rating. I can see why sentiment has improved, because the top-line recovery is getting broader and management is also pushing on cost actions and productivity tools. But I am worried consensus estimates may be too high, as the enterprise mix is still weighing on margins, Experis remains weak, and permanent hiring has not improved enough. I may miss the first leg of share price recovery if I am wrong, but I prefer to wait for another 1 to 2 quarters of similar/better adj. EPS growth before I turn more bullish.

data.title: Credo: The Market Hasn't Fully Priced In The DustPhotonics Deal's Implications
data.description:
Performance assessment

Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd (CRDO) has risen 41% in less than 2 weeks. It looks like my 'Strong Buy' upgrade was very timely:

Elevator pitch

In my last article on Credo, I noted how the company is foraying into the semiconductor optics/photonics market via its ZeroFlap (ZF) Optics product. Just 1 week later, Credo announced a major M&A deal that doubles down on this new product category bet. In this piece, I will analyze this deal and its implications for Credo:

  1. The DustPhotonics acquisition can boost Credo's revenues and margins.
  2. The deal is at an attractive transaction multiple.
  3. Credo's balance sheet can easily handle the upfront cash consideration.
  4. The relative valuation discount has narrowed but is still below the comps median.
  5. CRDO stock may take a breather here.

The DustPhotonics acquisition can boost Credo's revenues and margins

DustPhotonics produces Silicon Photonics Photonic Integrated Circuits (SiPho PICs). Let's break that down:

  • Silicon photonics means it uses light (photon particles) to move and process information on silicon chips. This is similar in concept to fiber optic cables, which move data around using light instead of copper wires.
  • Photonic integrated circuits (PICs) are the optical version of the electronic circuit.

SiPho PICs are used in optical transceivers, which convert between electrical signals and light signals in circuits. This is how it fits into Credo's optical components portfolio:

Credo's ZF Optics module (discussed in more depth in my last article) requires 2 key components:

  1. An optical digital signal processor (DSP) that generates and cleans up electrical signals.
  2. A PIC that converts between electrical and optical signals.

Credo already has the DSP capability in-house. But it currently buys PICs from other vendors. With the DustPhotonics acquisition, Credo not only adds a merchant PIC business from the sale of those PICs but also enables them to sell a more vertically integrated ZF Optics product. Overall, this is expected to lead to an overall optics revenue contribution of $500M in FY27 and an overall high revenue growth rate too.

Our fiscal '27 revenue is now expected to grow in excess of 75% year-over-year, and that's with a mid-single-digit sequential growth through the first half of the year and the second half inflection driven by that ramp of our optical portfolio, which is optical DSP, ZF optics, and now, our silicon photonics platform.

- CFO Daniel Fleming in the DustPhotonics M&A call.

Note that Credo has an April-ending fiscal year.

The Apr'26e number is formed by considering Credo's Q4 FY26 revenue guidance of $430M.

Note that the 75% YoY growth management is indicating for FY27 is not yet reflected in consensus revenue estimates, which are still penciling in 53%:

Hence, I see a case for revenue upside here.

I also expect the in-sourcing of the PIC capability to help reduce component costs:

Critically, SiPho PIC technology is a foundational component of Credo's ZeroFlap (ZF) Optical Transceiver platform. Bringing this capability in-house mitigates external supply dependencies, accelerates product development cycles, and creates a pathway to substantial cost structure improvement at volume.

- Credo M&A Press Release.

This can lead to an uptick in gross margins over the medium term to ~70% levels in my view:

The April 26 gross margin of 65% is based on management's guidance.

The deal is at an attractive transaction multiple

Credo will acquire DustPhotonics for upfront consideration of $750 million cash and approximately 0.92 million shares of Credo common stock... In addition, Credo may pay incremental contingent consideration of up to approximately 3.21 million shares based on the achievement of certain financial milestones.

- Credo Press Release.

Based on this, assuming the share price at the deal announcement on 13 Apr'26 of $134.64 and the current share price of $160.69 for the contingent shares consideration, I arrive at an EV consideration range of $874M—$1.39B.

Now, Credo's optical business before this acquisition was expected to contribute $300M in revenues in FY27. And this M&A deal is expected to lead to optical revenues of more than $500M in FY27. So based on this, I compute an implied EV/revenue multiple of 4.4-6.9x:

To see how this deal multiple compares, I have put together a short list of relevant transaction comps from Marvell Technology, Inc. (MRVL)—Inphi, Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO)—Acacia, Coherent Corp. (COHR)—II-VI, and Intel Corporation (INTC)—Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (TSEM) deals using company filings, press releases, and data from Capital IQ. I have also considered the relevant semiconductors' revenue multiple average based on data from FirstPageSage:

Putting this all together, I estimate that Credo's DustPhotonics acquisition has occurred at a 30.2% discount based on upfront consideration numbers and an 11% premium based on potential total consideration numbers:

The Intel-Tower Semiconductor deal never went through as it did not get the requisite regulatory approvals; however, I have still included it as I view it as a key information signal since there was no issue with the valuations.

Overall, I conclude that the DustPhotonics M&A is happening at an attractive transaction multiple. I am giving far more weight to the upfront consideration numbers, as the potential total consideration is likely to overstate the price paid, as it does not account for the additional revenue milestones that would be hit to activate that scenario. That detail is not disclosed publicly right now.

Credo's balance sheet can easily handle the upfront cash consideration

Before this deal announcement, Credo had amassed a good deal of liquidity over the past 2 quarters. So it has an ample amount remaining after the $750M upfront cash consideration outflow:

I wonder if the accumulation of total liquidity may be a leading indicator of an upcoming M&A deal for Credo. More data is needed to get a more reliable signal, but I think this means we should have the company's liquidity levels as a key monitorable going ahead.

The company's balance sheet remains very robust, as it will remain in a $454M net cash position even after this deal.

This allows the company to have the resources to continue funding growth investments itself and also bear some cyclicality.

The relative valuation discount narrowed but is still below the comps median

CRDO's 1-yr forward P/E of 36.3x is now at a 21.6% discount vs. the 46.3x median of its peers. This is a smaller relative valuation discount than 35.7% almost 2 weeks ago. So the market is clearly re-rating the stock.

To me, CRDO's valuation still seems attractive as it is still cheaper than many of its comps.

CRDO stock may take a breather here

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using technical analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

CRDO Technical Analysis

CRDO has moved up very sharply over the last couple of weeks with impressive bullish momentum. Now, the stock has reached a key resistance area from which the sellers printed a bearish engulfing as they pushed the stock down. So I am a little bit more cautious here. I expect a bit of a pause here during the broader rally up.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Bullish, as the purchase consideration for DustPhotonics is quite reasonable, and this business can boost both revenues and margins for Credo.
  • Valuation: Bullish, as Credo is still trading at a valuation discount compared to its comps.
  • Technical Analysis: Neutral as the bullish momentum faces a key resistance level.
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Bullish, Strong Buy.

data.title: AI Spending: The Biggest Gamble In U.S. History
data.description:
The market rallied hard to the upside last week on the news that Iran will allow open passage through the Strait of Hormuz during the current ceasefire. Whether traffic resumes normal transit through this global chokepoint will be the key to where equities head over the next few weeks. There already seems to be a major hiccup on this front.

The conflict in the Middle East has taken focus off myriad other economic concerns for investors. These include a moribund housing sector, anemic job growth over the past five quarters, rising CRE delinquency rates, and, of course, more and more 'cockroaches' emerging in private credit. There are few core economic drivers in the current economy. One is fiscal deficit spending. This has been running near $2 trillion annually in recent years.

Obviously, this is unsustainable over the longer term as the U.S. is barreling towards $40 trillion in federal debt. And that is before the massive increase in defense spending the administration has proposed for FY2027. However, the core growth driver of the U.S. economy right now remains the huge surge of technology spending due to the AI Revolution.

The country is making the biggest investment, or gamble, depending on your view, in U.S. history on this technology paradigm shift. I recently found the graph above that puts this investment in historical context. On an inflation-adjusted basis, AI infrastructure spending already dwarfs that of the Manhattan Project or Marshall Plan. By the end of 2026, the U.S. will have spent more on AI-related projects than on the construction of the Interstate Highway System, the railroad buildout, the effort to go to the moon, and the International Space Station combined. And this spending will have happened over just seven years (most of it during 2024-2026), compared to decades for other major infrastructure buildouts in American history.

Will this investment turn out to be well spent? Based on equity valuations, the market certainly is betting the AI Revolution will bring huge profits to those companies in the AI ecosystem and benefit U.S. society overall. But is that a correct assumption? In today's article, I outline four possible scenarios that could be brought into being by this massive AI buildout.

#1: The Birth of 'Skynet'

As someone who has watched each movie in the Terminator franchise at least a half dozen times over the decades, I will use 'Skynet' as a catchall phrase for the possibility that AI spawns unexpected and very negative outcomes. There have been many stories around AI 'hallucinating' and taking actions that trigger network outages and security breaches.

That said, I don't see AI becoming 'self-aware' and declaring war against the human race. That said, I do believe investors are substantially underestimating the growing ability of this technology to enable some potentially horrific outcomes. Combined with drone technology, AI is already changing the nature of the battlefield.

In coming years this will boost the ability for a country or collection of individuals to carry out asymmetrical warfare. For the cost of building a modern aircraft carrier, more than a half million $25,000 Shahed drones could be purchased or built. Combined with AI, the potential for drone swarms is immense. In addition, this fleet would take much less time to build and be much cheaper to maintain and deploy.

For a million dollars, in theory, a terrorist organization could gather 40 drones and hit important but poorly guarded facilities like data centers, refineries, and electrical substations. How long before an event like this occurs in the States and Europe? If these attacks start to become commonplace, how negative will this be for society, the economy, and the markets?

From an investment perspective, I expect a new investment theme to play out in coming years around war unicorns. These will be billion-dollar startups that mass-produce AI-enabled drones, laser systems to shoot down drones on an economical scale, produce glider munitions, and other functions/capabilities to enable/counteract this new form of warfare.

#2: Huge Capital Misallocation

In this scenario, hyperscalers like Meta Platforms (META) (who burned over $80 billion on the metaverse and VR to little effect) realize they are not getting an acceptable return on investment. They then start to cut their capital expenditure budgets, which the U.S. economy has become so dependent upon.

This could happen due to myriad reasons. AI-related revenues might not ramp up to the degree they need to justify these generational investments. Rising capex budgets have already drastically cut free cash flow at the hyperscalers. Many, like Oracle (ORCL), have issued tens of billions in debt to fund these efforts. Costs could spiral out of control as GPUs, which most major companies are now depreciating on five-to-six-year schedules, could need to be replaced every two or three years. Technological innovations could come along to radically reduce the cost of operating AI, making many of these massive AI data centers redundant.

Finally, supply chain gaps, transformer shortages, and grid constraints could slow down the AI buildout considerably. A recent report noted that 'nearly half of the roughly 16 gigawatts of U.S. data-center capacity slated to come online in 2026 will likely face delays or outright cancellation.' Projects covering only five gigawatts of capacity have broken ground. It also should be noted that local opposition to new data center projects is growing across the country. Partly due to rising electricity costs that many have blamed on these facilities.

#3: White-Collar Recession

In this scenario, the huge AI investment generates a sufficient return on investment and generates plenty of revenues for the likes of OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO), as well as the hyperscalers. U.S. productivity is boosted substantially as all sorts of businesses, big and small, integrate AI throughout their operational processes, lowering their costs significantly.

A 25-person law or accounting firm can now be run by 20 or even 15 people just as efficiently as it was prior to AI. The downside to all of this efficiency is a huge wave of unemployment. Many have projected AI could relatively quickly push the U.S. unemployment rate to 10%. Obviously, this would not be a positive for a consumer-led economy. Social cohesion in the U.S. could fracture even further. That is why many billionaires like Elon Musk are pushing the concept of universal basic income, or UBI.

#4: Utopia

In this scenario, the AI buildout generates sufficient return on investment to continue to boost earnings growth for the major players of the AI ecosystem. AI capabilities provide a huge boost to U.S. productivity. There are some disruptions to the labor markets, but new companies and industries get created that absorb most of these job losses. AI and robotics drastically reduce the costs of producing goods and act as an engine for deflation.

Conclusion

The market is tilting heavily towards scenario #4 playing out for the largest technology spending spree in U.S. history. I believe it is instructive to look at other major technology paradigm shifts in U.S. history. The buildout of the railroad networks greatly reduced the cost and time to move goods to market and facilitated mass transportation. Electricity made it possible to distribute energy efficiently, effectively, and everywhere. The birth of automobiles and trucking accomplished many of the same things as the building of the railroads and greatly increased the mobility of man, leading to the creation of suburbs in major cities along with numerous other impacts.

The birth of the internet massively increased connectivity, creating a digital superhighway. It is important to note that too much speculation during the railroad era led directly to the 1873 economic recession, one of the worst and longest-lasting contractions in U.S. history. The Internet boom eventually led to the Internet bust, which knocked off over 80% of value from the NASDAQ from peak to trough. Scores of automobile companies eventually went bankrupt, with only a few major manufacturers surviving.

Each of these paradigm shifts boosted U.S. productivity significantly and introduced a significant measure of disruption into the labor markets as well. However, they also created new industries and major new job opportunities. The birth of the internet laid the groundwork for Alphabet (GOOG), Expedia Group, Inc. (EXPE), Amazon (AMZN), and Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER) that created new positions and absorbed job losses elsewhere in the economy due to this new technology. These previous technology paradigm shifts also took place over a long time, decades in most cases.

But these technology paradigm shifts did not replace man but made him more productive. AI, especially when combined with robotics, has the potential to replace man in many respects. And this revolution is happening in years, not decades. Will the largest gamble in U.S. history be a winner for equities, society, and the economy? The markets certainly are betting that it will. However, I have my doubts this transition will be as smooth as the markets are assuming.

data.title: EPR Properties: My Top 'Must-Buy' REIT For 2026
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Life has a way of derailing plans. Have you ever planned a major event, like a wedding, birthday party, or corporate retreat, and then, as the event nears, things start going wrong? The power goes out at the venue, a storm comes in, the decorations are late, the caterer gets lost, and I thought YOU were going to pick up the cake!

It's just a reality of life that people can't predict all the things that will go wrong in the future. Of course, in the future, we'll be looking back and stating that it was obvious what was going to happen.

In three months, everyone will be a genius about what trades you should have made three months ago. People will be proclaiming that they “knew” that the Iran war was going to escalate and oil was going to go up to $200/barrel, or that they “knew” a peace deal would be reached and that the Strait of Hormuz would be open for business, and oil is headed back to $70/barrel.

We forget how quickly the world can change in just three months. Remember January 2020? REITs were soaring high, the stock market was strong, and investors were optimistic about the future. The talking heads on TV were shouting, “Buy, Buy, Buy!”

Three months later, the sky was falling, COVID-19 was shutting everything down, and the mayhem and economic damage were going to be unprecedented. “Sell! Sell! Sell!”

Today, we want to take a closer look at a REIT that had its plans materially derailed by COVID-19. It had just sold a large portion of its portfolio and was looking to deploy capital into properties where people congregated in large groups.

EPR Properties—Yield 6.6%

EPR Properties (EPR) is an equity REIT that is focused on investing in experiential real estate. These are properties where people go to do things. Coming into the year, we were bullish on EPR because we viewed it as a holding that was returning to growth mode.

COVID-19 threw a monkey wrench into EPR's plans as a huge portion of its tenants were unable to pay rent. After all, EPR owns properties where people go to do things in groups with other people—not the best businesses in a social distancing world.

EPR battened down the hatches. Suspending its common dividend and sitting on cash while it waited for its tenants to recover. EPR worked with tenants to defer rent and spent about three years collecting that back rent.

Minimal Acquisitions

During COVID-19, EPR wasn't in a position to deploy a lot of capital. In the aftermath of COVID-19, rapidly rising interest rates discouraged leveraging up and expansion. EPR focused on recovering the unpaid back rent and waited for its cost of capital to improve. Acquisitions were slow and deliberate.

The result has been a slow recovery to pre-COVID levels, as opposed to growth. Late last year, management indicated they were ready to get aggressive with acquisitions. This was backed up with their Q4 guidance: Source.

EPR is targeting $400-$500 million in new investments, against just $25-$75 million in dispositions. Sometimes, investing can be complicated, and there are a lot of factors that have to be considered. Other times, it's remarkably straightforward. In this case, owning more properties results in more rent and, therefore, more money. It really is as simple as a bigger portfolio = more FFO.

A Bird In The Hand

The largest question at Q4 earnings was whether management would be able to deliver on their stated goals. Well, shortly after earnings, EPR announced it was acquiring 7 amusement/water parks in a $315 million acquisition, EPR's largest portfolio acquisition since 2017.

The acquisition from Six Flags Entertainment Corporation (FUN) includes six U.S. properties and one Canadian property:

These properties fall solidly into EPR's wheelhouse, as attractions are its third-largest category. This will represent approximately a 25% increase in EPR's exposure to this category. Source.

Underwriting was at 2.0x rent coverage, which is consistent with EPR's existing portfolio.

In addition to the $34 million in acquisitions closed in Q1, EPR has acquired over 85% of its announced acquisition targets for the year, and it is only April. That tells us that it is extremely likely that EPR will be increasing guidance at its Q1 earnings report. We believe it is very unlikely that EPR is going to just sit back and stop acquiring for the rest of the year.

Conclusion

The bottom line is that a larger portfolio will drive earnings higher. EPR has already closed on 6 of the 7 properties and expects the Canadian property to close later in Q2. We are confident that EPR has other irons in the fire and will be increasing its acquisition guidance, which will also lead to an increase in FFOAA (Funds From Operations As Adjusted) guidance for 2026.

While we can't predict the future, we can see what has already happened. We see a company that is ahead of schedule and is growing faster than management telegraphed at Q4 earnings, based on announced and closed transactions.

EPR is trading at only 10x FFOAA based on prior guidance. An increase in guidance and an increased expected growth rate should drive the price higher and inspire the market to trade EPR at a higher multiple. We believe fair value for EPR today is 14x FFOAA, which would be over $77/share.
